---
title: "Linear systems in R; solution spaces"
subtitle: "Day 2 — Quantitative biology"
author: "Quantitative biology and integrative omics"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, ../theme.scss]
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    transition: slide
    transition-speed: fast
    highlight-style: github
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-line-numbers: false
    incremental: false
    chalkboard: false
    menu: true
---

## About this lecture

Yesterday we solved small systems $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ by hand. Today we
hand the work to R — and discover that not every system has a tidy answer. You
will be able to:

- Build $A$ and $\mathbf{b}$ in R and solve with
  [`solve`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/solve.html).
- Recognise the **three kinds of solution space**: exactly one, none, or
  infinitely many.
- Connect each kind to a **deconvolution** scenario: exactly-, over-, or
  under-determined.
- Know what to reach for when $A$ is singular or non-square: least squares,
  via [`lm`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/lm.html) (full treatment later).

::: notes
Today is the R day. The key conceptual jump is that "solve a linear system" is
not always a well-posed request — there may be no answer, or a whole family of
answers. We tie that to whether a cell-type deconvolution problem has enough,
too few, or conflicting measurements. Keep the code minimal and the intuition
front and centre.
:::

## Recap: the deconvolution problem

A bulk expression sample is a mixture. Known per-cell-type **signatures**
(matrix $A$, one column per cell type) and the measured **bulk profile**
(vector $\mathbf{b}$) give a system
$$
A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b},
$$
whose solution $\mathbf{x}$ is the **fractions** (or amounts) of each cell
type. Today: what if the system cannot be solved exactly?

::: notes
Recall the running example. The number of measured genes is the number of
equations (rows of A); the number of cell types is the number of unknowns
(columns of A). The relationship between those two counts is what drives
everything today.
:::

## Building $A$ and $\mathbf{b}$ in R {.smaller}

```r
# Two cell types, two marker genes
A <- matrix(c(2, 1,
              1, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
b <- c(8, 9)

A
#      [,1] [,2]
# [1,]    2    1
# [2,]    1    3
```

- [`matrix`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matrix.html) with `byrow = TRUE` fills row
  by row, so the code reads like the equations on paper.
- `nrow` sets the number of rows; `c(...)` is just the entries.
- Default `byrow = FALSE` fills **column by column** — a classic bug.

::: notes
Spend a moment on byrow because it bites everyone once. The default fills down
the first column, so a matrix typed left-to-right comes out transposed unless
you set byrow = TRUE. Always print the matrix and eyeball it against your
equations before trusting it.
:::

## Solving with `solve()` {.smaller}

```r
x <- solve(A, b)
x
# [1] 3 2          # 3 units tumour, 2 units immune

# Always check: does A x reproduce b?
A %*% x
#      [,1]
# [1,]    8
# [2,]    9
```

- [`solve(A, b)`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/solve.html) returns the
  $\mathbf{x}$ with $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ — it runs Gaussian elimination,
  exactly what you did by hand on day 1.
- [`%*%`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matmult.html) is matrix multiplication. Use it
  to verify, just like substituting back on paper.

::: notes
solve is the workhorse. Two arguments means "solve this system". Note that it
returns the same 3, 2 we found by hand yesterday. The verification step with
the matrix product is the R equivalent of plugging back into the original
equations — make it a reflex.
:::

## `solve(A)` alone gives the inverse {.smaller}

```r
Ainv <- solve(A)        # one argument: the inverse of A
Ainv %*% b
# [1] 3 2               # same answer, x = A^{-1} b
```

- `solve(A, b)` is preferred over `solve(A) %*% b`: it is faster and more
  numerically accurate (it never forms the inverse explicitly).
- The inverse $A^{-1}$ only exists when $A$ is **square and non-singular** —
  precisely the "exactly one solution" case below.

::: notes
Worth showing so they recognise both forms in the wild, but steer them toward
the two-argument solve. Forming the inverse is wasteful and less stable. Flag
that the inverse existing is equivalent to the system having a unique solution
— this links directly into the next slides.
:::

## Three kinds of solution space

For $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ there are exactly three possibilities:

1. **Exactly one** solution — the system is *consistent and determined*.
2. **No** solution — the equations *contradict* each other.
3. **Infinitely many** solutions — the equations are *consistent but
   leave freedom*.

The next slides take each in turn and pair it with a deconvolution scenario.

::: notes
This trichotomy is a theorem: a linear system has zero, one, or infinitely many
solutions — never, say, exactly two. Geometrically (day 3) each equation is a
line or plane, and we are asking how they intersect. For now, anchor each case
to the biology.
:::

## Case 1 — exactly one (well-determined) {.smaller}

As many independent measurements as unknowns. $A$ is **square** and
**non-singular**.

Biology: #genes measured = #cell types, and the signatures are genuinely
different. The deconvolution has a single answer.

```r
A <- matrix(c(2, 1,
              1, 3), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
solve(A, c(8, 9))
# [1] 3 2          # the unique solution
```

This is the happy case from day 1. `solve()` just returns the answer.

::: notes
This is the case students already understand from yesterday. Stress the two
conditions: square (equations equal unknowns) and non-singular (the equations
are genuinely independent, not rephrasings of each other). When both hold,
there is one and only one solution and solve returns it.
:::

## Case 2 — no solution (over-determined) {.smaller}

More equations than unknowns, and they **conflict**. Often $A$ is **non-square**
(more rows than columns).

Biology: we measured *more* genes than cell types. With real noisy data the
equations rarely agree exactly, so **no** $\mathbf{x}$ fits all of them.

```r
A <- matrix(c(1, 0,
              0, 1,
              1, 1), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)   # 3 eqns, 2 unknowns
b <- c(2, 3, 6)                                # but 2 + 3 = 5, not 6
solve(A, b)
# Error in solve.default(A, b) : 'a' must be a square matrix
```

`solve()` refuses: it only handles square systems.

::: notes
Over-determined means too many equations. With exact data they might still all
agree, but with measurement noise they generally do not — there is no x that
satisfies every equation at once. solve flatly errors on a non-square A. The
fix, least squares, is two slides away. Note this is actually the realistic
omics case: thousands of genes, a handful of cell types.
:::

## Case 3 — infinitely many (under-determined) {.smaller}

Fewer independent equations than unknowns. $A$ is **singular** (or has fewer
rows than columns).

Biology: we measured *too few* genes to distinguish the cell types — many
fraction combinations explain the data equally well.

```r
A <- matrix(c(1, 1,
              2, 2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)   # row 2 = 2 x row 1
b <- c(3, 6)
solve(A, b)
# Error in solve.default(...) : Lapack routine dgesv: system is exactly singular
```

Both equations say the same thing ($x_1 + x_2 = 3$), so any $\mathbf{x}$ on
that line works. `solve()` errors on the **singular** matrix.

::: notes
Under-determined means too few independent equations. Here row two is just
twice row one — no new information — so the matrix is singular and there is a
whole line of solutions. solve detects the singularity and stops. The biology:
not enough markers to resolve the cell types. Contrast the two error messages
on this and the previous slide so they can diagnose which case they hit.
:::

## Detecting which case you are in {.smaller}

```r
dim(A)                 # rows (equations) vs cols (unknowns)
det(A)                 # square only: ~0 means singular
qr(A)$rank             # number of independent equations
```

- `dim` non-square $\Rightarrow$ over- or under-determined by shape.
- [`det`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/det.html) $\approx 0$ (square) $\Rightarrow$
  **singular** $\Rightarrow$ not Case 1.
- [`qr`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/qr.html)`$rank` < #unknowns $\Rightarrow$ the
  equations do not pin down every unknown.

A square $A$ with $\det A \ne 0$ and full rank is exactly Case 1.

::: notes
Give them a diagnostic toolkit. dim tells you the shape. det near zero flags a
singular square matrix — but careful, floating point means it is rarely exactly
zero, so "small relative to the entries" is the real test. Rank is the most
robust single number: full rank equal to the number of unknowns is the unique-
solution condition.
:::

## When there is no exact answer: least squares {.smaller}

For over-determined systems (Case 2) we cannot satisfy every equation, so we
find the $\mathbf{x}$ that comes **closest** — minimising the total squared
mismatch $\lVert A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}\rVert^2$. This is **least squares**.

```r
A <- matrix(c(1, 0,
              0, 1,
              1, 1), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
b <- c(2, 3, 6)

qr.solve(A, b)            # least-squares solution
# [1] 2.333333 3.333333

coef(lm(b ~ A - 1))       # same thing, via linear regression
```

[`qr.solve`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/qr.html) and
[`lm`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/lm.html) both give the best-fit $\mathbf{x}$.
We treat this properly later in the course.

::: notes
This is the bridge to the rest of the course. When you cannot fit everything,
fit as well as possible by minimising squared error — that is least squares,
and it is what lm does under the hood. Mention it lightly here; the geometry
(projection, day 3) and the full regression treatment come later. The takeaway:
an over-determined deconvolution is solved by least squares, not solve.
:::

## A note for real deconvolution {.smaller}

Real cell-type fractions must be **non-negative** and usually **sum to 1**.
Plain `solve`/`lm` know nothing about that and can return negative fractions.

- Production tools (e.g. CIBERSORT, and Bioconductor packages) add these
  constraints (non-negative least squares, support-vector regression, etc.).
- The linear-system core is the same — the constraints are extra structure on
  top.

We use the unconstrained version to learn the mechanics; the constrained
version is the real tool.

::: notes
Honesty about what we defer, per the course design principles. The bare linear
algebra can hand you a negative fraction, which is biologically nonsense. Real
deconvolution adds constraints. We deliberately strip those away to expose the
linear-system skeleton students need to learn first. Name-drop the real tools
so they know where to look.
:::

## Summary {.smaller}

- Build systems in R with [`matrix`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matrix.html)
  (`byrow = TRUE`!) and solve with
  [`solve(A, b)`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/solve.html); check with
  [`%*%`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/matmult.html).
- A linear system has **one**, **none**, or **infinitely many** solutions —
  matching well-, over-, and under-determined deconvolution.
- Diagnose with [`dim`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/dim.html),
  [`det`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/det.html),
  [`qr`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/qr.html)`$rank`.
- Over-determined $\Rightarrow$ **least squares**
  ([`qr.solve`](https://rdrr.io/r/base/qr.html),
  [`lm`](https://rdrr.io/r/stats/lm.html)); full treatment later.

**Next:** Day 3 — the geometric picture: vectors, dot products, and projection
(which is *why* least squares works).

::: notes
Pull the threads together: the R mechanics, the three solution spaces tied to
the biology, the diagnostics, and the least-squares escape hatch. Preview day 3,
where the geometry explains the least-squares idea we waved at today. Point them
to the exercise to practise all three cases in R.
:::
